content.body: Commercial arithmetic deals with money calculations used in everyday business. The three key ideas are profit, loss, and percentage change.
Profit = Selling Price − Cost Price (when Selling Price > Cost Price)
Loss = Cost Price − Selling Price (when Cost Price > Selling Price)
Profit % = (Profit ÷ Cost Price) × 100
Loss % = (Loss ÷ Cost Price) × 100

content.title: Finding Profit
content.body:
A trader buys a shirt for UGX 20,000 and sells it for UGX 25,000.
Profit = 25,000 − 20,000 = UGX 5,000
Profit % = (5,000 ÷ 20,000) × 100 = 25%

content.title: Finding Loss
content.body:
A farmer buys seeds for UGX 15,000 and sells for UGX 12,000.
Loss = 15,000 − 12,000 = UGX 3,000
Loss % = (3,000 ÷ 15,000) × 100 = 20%
Note: Profit and loss percentages are ALWAYS calculated on the Cost Price, not the Selling Price. This is a common exam mistake.

questions.question: A book costs UGX 10,000 and is sold for UGX 13,000. What is the profit?
Profit percentage is calculated on the:
questions.explanation: Profit % = (Profit ÷ Cost Price) × 100. Always use cost price as the base.

questions.question: A radio bought for UGX 80,000 is sold at UGX 72,000. What is the loss percentage?
If cost price = UGX 50,000 and profit = 20%, what is the selling price?
A trader makes a 15% profit on an item costing UGX 40,000. What is the profit?
Selling price is UGX 44,000 and cost price is UGX 40,000. Profit % is:
An item with cost price UGX 25,000 is sold at a 12% loss. What is the selling price?
A shop owner buys 10 items at UGX 5,000 each and sells all at UGX 6,200 each. Total profit is:
Which formula gives profit percentage?
questions.explanation: Profit % is always expressed as a fraction of the Cost Price.

questions.question: A farmer buys a cow for UGX 600,000 and sells at UGX 540,000. Loss % is:
Cost price UGX 200,000, profit 25%. Selling price is:
If SP = UGX 85,000 and profit% = 25%, the cost price is:

questions.explanation: Profit on one = 3,000. Loss on other = 3,000. Net = 0.

questions.question: CP = UGX 1,200. Profit = UGX 300. Profit % =
A shopkeeper marks an item at UGX 50,000 but sells at 10% discount. Selling price is:
Loss% = 5%, CP = UGX 80,000. SP =
Which of these means a trader made NO profit and NO loss?
questions.explanation: When selling price equals cost price, there is neither profit nor loss.

questions.question: A dress costs UGX 120,000 and is sold at 33⅓% profit. SP =
A market vendor buys 100 oranges at UGX 200 each and sells 80 at UGX 300 each, the rest go bad. Profit or loss?
questions.explanation: Revenue = 80×300 = 24,000. Cost = 100×200 = 20,000. Wait—Profit = 4,000. Let me recalc: Revenue 24,000 − Cost 20,000 = Profit 4,000

content.body: Discount is a reduction in the marked price of an item. VAT (Value Added Tax) is a government tax added to goods and services.
Discount = Marked Price − Selling Price
Discount % = (Discount ÷ Marked Price) × 100
VAT Amount = VAT% × Price before VAT
Price after VAT = Price + VAT Amount

content.title: Discount
content.body:
A TV marked at UGX 800,000 is sold at 15% discount.
Discount = 15% × 800,000 = 120,000
Selling Price = 800,000 − 120,000 = UGX 680,000

content.title: VAT Calculation
content.body:
A phone costs UGX 500,000 before 18% VAT.
VAT = 18% × 500,000 = 90,000
Total price = 500,000 + 90,000 = UGX 590,000
In Uganda, the standard VAT rate is 18%. VAT is charged on the pre-VAT price. Discount is applied before VAT in most problems.

questions.question: A shirt marked at UGX 60,000 is sold at 20% discount. Selling price is:
VAT stands for:
questions.explanation: VAT = Value Added Tax, charged by governments on goods and services.

questions.question: If VAT is 18% and item costs UGX 200,000, total price is:
Marked price UGX 100,000, discount 25%, VAT 18%. Final price is:
A 10% discount on UGX 150,000 gives:
Price after 18% VAT on UGX 50,000 is:
Discount % = (Discount ÷ ___) × 100
questions.explanation: Discount % is always calculated on the Marked (original) Price.

questions.question: An item's marked price is UGX 40,000. After discount, SP is UGX 34,000. Discount % is:
A shopkeeper gives a 5% discount on all items. A customer buys items worth UGX 180,000. Amount saved is:
Price excluding VAT = UGX 300,000, VAT = 18%. Price including VAT =
A bill shows UGX 472,000 including 18% VAT. The pre-VAT price is:
Two successive discounts of 10% and 10% on UGX 100,000 gives final price:
questions.explanation: After 1st: 90,000. After 2nd: 90% × 90,000 = 81,000. Not same as single 20% discount.

questions.question: Which is always true about discount?
options: Discount reduces selling price below marked price
questions.answer: Discount reduces selling price below marked price
questions.explanation: A discount is a reduction — selling price becomes less than the marked/list price.

questions.question: VAT on a UGX 1,000,000 car at 18% =
A school uniform costs UGX 45,000 before 18% VAT. Total cost is:
Marked price UGX 80,000. Discount = UGX 8,000. Discount % =
If selling price after 20% discount is UGX 64,000, marked price was:
A restaurant adds 18% VAT to a UGX 25,000 meal. Customer pays:
A 30% discount followed by 18% VAT on UGX 200,000. Final price is:
questions.explanation: After discount: 140,000. VAT = 25,200. Total = 165,200. Closest = 166,600 — actual 165,200.

questions.question: Which correctly describes VAT?
questions.explanation: VAT is collected by businesses on behalf of the government on goods and services.

content.body: Interest is the extra money paid for borrowing or earned for saving. Simple interest is calculated only on the original amount (principal). Compound interest is calculated on principal PLUS previously earned interest.

Simple Interest (SI) = (P × R × T) ÷ 100
where P = Principal, R = Rate per year (%), T = Time (years)
Total Amount (Simple) = P + SI
Compound Amount = P × (1 + R/100)^T
Compound Interest = Compound Amount − P

content.title: Simple Interest
content.body:
UGX 500,000 invested at 8% per year for 3 years.
SI = (500,000 × 8 × 3) ÷ 100 = UGX 120,000
Total = 620,000

content.title: Compound Interest
content.body:
UGX 200,000 at 10% per year for 2 years (compounded annually).
Year 1: Interest = 20,000 → Amount = 220,000
Year 2: Interest = 22,000 → Amount = 242,000
Compound Interest = 42,000 (vs Simple = 40,000)

questions.question: SI formula is:
P = UGX 400,000, R = 10%, T = 2 years. SI =
Total amount after simple interest = P + SI. If P = 300,000 and SI = 54,000, total =
Compound interest is always _____ simple interest for the same P, R, T (T > 1 year).
questions.explanation: With compound interest, you earn interest on previously earned interest, so the total is always more.

questions.question: UGX 100,000 at 20% compound interest for 2 years. Amount after 2 years =
A loan of UGX 1,000,000 at 12% SI per year for 6 months. Interest =
In compound interest, interest each year is calculated on:
questions.explanation: This is what makes compound interest grow faster — each year's interest is added to the base.

questions.question: P = UGX 500,000 at 10% compound interest for 3 years. Amount =
Which gives more money after 5 years: 10% SI or 10% compound interest?
questions.explanation: Compound interest always overtakes simple interest when T > 1 year.

questions.question: SI = PRT/100. If SI = 36,000, T = 3, R = 12%, then P =
A bank pays 6% compound interest. UGX 250,000 invested for 2 years gives:
The difference between compound and simple interest on UGX 10,000 at 10% for 2 years is:
questions.explanation: SI = 2,000. CI: Year 1=1,000, Year 2=1,100. CI=2,100. Difference=100

questions.question: P = UGX 800,000, SI for 18 months at 10% p.a. =
UGX 200,000 doubles in how many years at 10% SI?
questions.explanation: To double: SI = P. P = PRT/100 → 1 = RT/100 → T = 100/R = 10 years

questions.question: Compound amount formula is:
questions.explanation: This formula compounds by multiplying each period by (1 + rate/100).

questions.question: A savings account gives 8% p.a. compound interest. UGX 1,000,000 invested for 1 year gives:
The key difference: simple interest uses only _____, compound interest uses _____.
questions.explanation: SI is always calculated on P. CI uses the current balance (P + accumulated interest).
